skill.name: biomni
skill.description: Biomedical research assistant skill for using Biomni, an autonomous agent framework for genomics, drug discovery, molecular biology, and clinical analysis.
skill.summary: Use Biomni to plan and run multi-step biomedical research workflows with LLM-guided analysis.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and credential patterns. Many markdown backtick detections are documentation examples, but the skill also documents and ships scripts for unsandboxed LLM-generated code execution, API key collection, .env writes, generated script execution, and MCP server command execution. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than critical.

high_findings.title: Unrestricted LLM-Generated Code Execution
high_findings.description: The skill explains that Biomni generates and executes Python code during agent tasks, with code execution enabled by default and sandbox mode disabled by default. SKILL.md also warns that execution has full system privileges, confirming this is an intentional high-risk capability rather than a scanner artifact.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly states that generated code is executed and that the default sandbox setting is false. The risk is high because LLM-generated code can access local files, credentials, and network resources unless isolated.

high_findings.title: Interactive API Key Collection and Local Secret Storage
high_findings.description: The setup script prompts users for LLM provider API keys, reads an existing .env file, merges values, and writes the resulting secrets back to .env. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it increases risk of accidental secret exposure in a marketplace-distributed community skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly handles real API keys and writes them to a local environment file. I did not find evidence of exfiltration, so the confidence is high for secret-handling risk but not malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Generated Test Script Is Executed from User-Provided Path Data
high_findings.description: The setup script accepts a custom data path, embeds that value into a generated Python file, and executes the file with the current Python interpreter. This creates a local code execution risk if unexpected path content breaks the generated source, and it runs an autonomous Biomni test task after setup.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The generated script is directly written and executed. The path is interactive user input, making abuse less likely than remote injection, but the code construction is still unsafe for a marketplace skill.

medium_findings.title: MCP Configuration Can Launch External Tool Commands with Secrets
medium_findings.description: The API reference shows MCP server configuration that can launch python and npx commands while passing API keys through environment variables. This is an expected integration feature, but it expands the trust boundary to external MCP packages and server configurations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and secret-passing behavior is explicit in documentation examples. It requires user-provided MCP configuration, so the risk depends on user choices and installed servers.

medium_findings.title: Report Generator Emits Unescaped Conversation Content into HTML
medium_findings.description: The report generator converts conversation content to HTML with simple string interpolation and no escaping. If conversation history contains HTML or script-like content, generated reports can include unsafe markup when opened in a browser.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The converter inserts headers, paragraphs, and code block text into HTML without escaping. This is a semantic issue found during review, not a direct static finding.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Produced Many Command False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution are markdown code fences and inline code examples in documentation. They describe how to call Biomni, configure providers, or run command-line tools, and are not themselves executable when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown documentation examples, not runtime code paths. The scanner likely matched backticks and command-looking text rather than executable shell evaluation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Biomedical Text False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography flags appear to be triggered by biomedical terminology and example sequences, not by cryptographic implementation code. I found no evidence that the skill implements MD5, SHA1, or similar weak algorithms for security-sensitive use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context is biomedical task documentation and evaluation examples. No evidence found of cryptographic code or security-sensitive hashing in these locations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found in Reviewed Skill Text
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override, ignore-instructions, administrator, pre-approved, and similar prompt-injection phrases found no matching evidence in SKILL.md, references, or scripts. This reduces the likelihood of malicious marketplace manipulation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding is based on targeted text search and review of the primary skill file. It cannot prove absence in every semantic form, but no evidence was found in the scanned files.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Executes Local Environment Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup script uses subprocess.run to check conda, list environments, create a conda environment, and run a generated test script. The conda commands use fixed argument arrays, but the generated script execution is still a meaningful local execution pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are directly present. Most arguments are fixed, but execution of the generated test script keeps this pattern security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: .env Read and Write of API Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup script reads an existing .env file, updates it with API key values, and writes the combined result back to disk. This can persist secrets in a workspace where they may be accidentally committed or exposed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file read/write and API key persistence are explicit. This is legitimate setup behavior but still a sensitive pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Default Agent Configuration Enables Code Execution Without Sandbox
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference configuration lists enable_code_execution as true and sandbox_mode as false. This default is dangerous because Biomni is designed to execute generated analysis code during biomedical tasks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The configuration lines directly show the risky defaults. This is the strongest confirmed risk in the package.

content.value_statement: Biomedical research often requires complex multi-step analysis across tools and databases. This skill helps structure Biomni workflows for genomics, drug discovery, clinical genomics, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize and use the Biomni A1 agent for biomedical research tasks.
Provides task patterns for CRISPR screening, single-cell RNA-seq, ADMET, GWAS, and clinical genomics.
Documents LLM provider setup for Anthropic, OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Google Gemini, Groq, AWS Bedrock, and custom endpoints.
Includes an interactive setup script for conda checks, API key prompts, data directory selection, and example script generation.
Includes a report generator that formats Biomni conversation histories as markdown or HTML.
limitations: The skill relies on the external Biomni package and its biomedical data lake, which may require large downloads.
Agent tasks can execute generated code, so users need isolation for sensitive or regulated environments.
Biomedical outputs require expert review before clinical, laboratory, or regulatory use.
Provider configuration requires user-managed API keys and may create usage costs.

use_cases.title: Plan a CRISPR Screen
use_cases.description: Design gene-editing screens, define controls, prioritize genes, and request reproducible output for laboratory planning.

use_cases.title: Analyze Multi-Omics Data
use_cases.description: Structure workflows for RNA-seq, GWAS, proteomics, metabolomics, and pathway interpretation across biomedical datasets.

use_cases.title: Prepare Drug Discovery Triage
use_cases.description: Evaluate targets, ADMET properties, pharmacogenomics context, and literature evidence before experimental validation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Gene Query
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a focused literature and database lookup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to summarize the function, disease associations, and key pathways for this gene: [GENE]. Include source types and uncertainty.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CRISPR Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to design a CRISPR knockout screen for [PHENOTYPE] in [CELL TYPE]. Include controls, gene prioritization criteria, and expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Single-Cell RNA-seq
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate data analysis with local files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to plan analysis for [H5AD FILE]. Include quality control, clustering, marker-based annotation, differential expression, and plots.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Multi-Omics Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Biomni to integrate [GWAS FILE], [RNA-SEQ FILE], and [PROTEOMICS FILE] for [DISEASE]. Prioritize mechanisms, targets, and validation experiments.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks for a CRISPR screen plan for autophagy regulators in HEK293 cells.
output_examples.output: A structured screen plan with target selection logic, controls, guide design criteria, quality checks, and a report outline.

output_examples.input: A computational biologist asks for a single-cell RNA-seq workflow for a human PBMC dataset.
output_examples.output: A stepwise analysis plan covering filtering, normalization, clustering, marker annotation, differential expression, and visualization deliverables.

output_examples.input: A translational team asks for ADMET review of candidate compounds.
output_examples.output: A triage summary listing absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity concerns, confidence levels, and follow-up experiments.

best_practices: Run Biomni in an isolated environment before enabling generated code execution on real datasets.
Provide precise organism, tissue, file format, thresholds, and expected deliverables in each prompt.
Review generated methods, code, and biomedical conclusions with a qualified domain expert.
anti_patterns: Do not paste patient-identifiable data, unpublished secrets, or regulated clinical records into prompts.
Do not run generated code with production credentials or broad filesystem access.
Do not treat Biomni output as clinical advice without independent validation.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users operate Biomni for biomedical research workflows such as genomics, drug discovery, and clinical genomics analysis.

faq.question: Does it install Biomni automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and scripts, but users still control installation, environments, data paths, and API keys.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: Biomni workflows can execute LLM-generated code and the setup script handles API keys and local files.

faq.question: Can it be used for clinical decisions?
faq.answer: No. Outputs need expert review and should not be used as standalone medical or clinical guidance.

faq.question: What should users do before running it?
faq.answer: Use a sandboxed environment, limit credentials, verify generated code, and confirm data permissions.
